title: SEGMENT 15 — Two-headed antibodies — bispecifics and BiTEs

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Two-headed antibodies — bispecifics and BiTEs

Here is another innovation that comes from the realization that, since we can engineer antibodies at the level of DNA, there is no fundamental requirement that an antibody have two identical Fab arms.
Both arms could grip the same target — that's a normal antibody.
But what if you engineered an antibody where one arm grips one target and the other arm grips a completely different target?
That's a bispecific antibody.
Bi for two, specific because each arm has a different specificity.
And it opens up therapeutic strategies that are simply impossible with normal antibodies.
Here's the analogy.
A normal antibody is like a piece of tape that's sticky on one type of surface — it can grab and hold one target.
A bispecific antibody is double-sided sticky tape — sticky on one side for one surface, sticky on the other side for a different surface.
You can use it to physically glue two things together that wouldn't otherwise be in contact.
The most successful application of this idea, by far, is to forcibly bring a killer T cell into contact with a cancer cell that the T cell otherwise wouldn't notice.
The drug class is called BiTE — Bispecific T-cell Engager.
One arm of the molecule grips a marker on the cancer cell.
The other arm grips CD3, a signaling component of the T cell receptor on the surface of all T cells.
When the BiTE binds both — cancer cell on one side, T cell on the other — it physically tethers them together, and the act of engaging CD3 strongly activates the T cell at that contact point.
The T cell, now believing it has encountered a target it should attack, dumps cytotoxic granules onto the cell directly across from it — which is the cancer cell.
The T cell doesn't have to recognize anything about the cancer; the bispecific has handed it the target.
The first approved BiTE was blinatumomab, for a B cell leukemia.
The two arms grip CD19 (on the leukemic B cells) and CD3 (on T cells).
The drug brings T cells into intimate contact with leukemia cells, and the T cells do the killing.
Response rates in patients who had failed other therapies were striking.
Several other BiTEs have followed, with more in development.
Note the conceptual move that bispecifics make.
With a checkpoint inhibitor, we removed an obstacle that was preventing T cells from killing cancer.
With a BiTE, we go further — we deliver the cancer to the T cell, providing the recognition signal the cancer was trying to hide.
You don't even need the T cell to know what the cancer is.
You just need it to be brought into the right place at the right time, with the right activating signal, and you can make essentially any T cell attack essentially any tagged target.
The antibody is performing the matchmaking the immune system failed to do.
Engineering challenges for bispecifics are real.
The molecule has to hold together (two arms with different specificities normally don't naturally pair) and the half-life can be short, especially for BiTEs that lack an Fc region.
There are now dozens of different bispecific formats being developed — different molecular architectures all trying to combine specificities elegantly.
The field is innovating fast, and bispecifics are expanding into autoimmune disease, infectious disease, and beyond oncology.
The general principle of using one antibody to bridge two different molecules is too powerful to limit to one therapeutic area.
